title: The Crocodile Relative That Switched from Four Legs to Two as It Grew Up
summary:
What if you started life crawling on all fours, but by the time you were an adult, you walked upright on two legs? That's exactly what a newly described prehistoric reptile did, and it's baffling scientists.

Meet Sonselasuchus cedrus, a crocodile relative that lived about 225 to 201 million years ago during the Late Triassic period. It was small—roughly the size of a poodle, standing about 25 inches tall. But its most surprising feature was its growth pattern. According to a study published in the Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology, researchers from the University of Washington and the Burke Museum analyzed fossil leg bones and found something unusual: the hind limbs grew much longer and more robust than the forelimbs as the animal aged.

"We think that Sonselasuchus had more proportional forelimbs and hindlimbs as young, and their hindlimb grew longer and more robust through adulthood," explained lead author Elliott Armour Smith, a graduate student. "Essentially, we think these creatures started out their lives on four legs... they then started walking on two legs as they grew up. This is particularly peculiar."

The fossils were discovered in 2014 at Petrified Forest National Park in Arizona, where a massive excavation has unearthed over 3,000 bones. The creature had a toothless beak, large eye sockets, and hollow bones—features that make it look remarkably similar to ornithomimid dinosaurs, which lived alongside it. But here's the twist: these traits evolved independently in two different groups. "Although similar to the ornithomimid dinosaurs, these features would have evolved separately," Armour Smith said. "This similarity was probably due to the fact that croc-line and bird-line archosaurs evolved in the same ecosystems and converged upon similar ecological roles."

In other words, nature sometimes comes up with the same brilliant design twice, even in completely different branches of the family tree. And Sonselasuchus is a perfect example of that.
why_it_matters: This discovery challenges simple ideas about how reptiles evolved and shows that nature can invent the same solution twice, which is important for understanding evolutionary biology.

keywords.explanation: A newly described reptile from the Late Triassic, a relative of crocodiles.
The back legs of the animal, which grew much longer as it aged.
The front legs, which remained more proportional as the animal grew.
Dinosaurs that looked like ostriches and also had toothless beaks and long legs.
The group of reptiles that includes crocodiles, dinosaurs, birds, and their extinct relatives.
When two different groups evolve similar features separately, not from a common ancestor.

questions.question: During which time period did Sonselasuchus live?
What body part grew the most as Sonselasuchus aged?
Which group of dinosaurs did scientists compare Sonselasuchus to because of its similar features?
What did the researchers say about the similarity between Sonselasuchus and those dinosaurs?
options: it means they are close relatives
it is a coincidence with no explanation
it proves they lived at the same time

questions.question: How many bones have been unearthed from that site so far?

background_read: The Late Triassic period saw the rise of the first dinosaurs and many other reptile groups. It ended about 201 million years ago with a major extinction event.

Convergent evolution happens when different species develop similar traits because they live in similar environments, not because they are closely related. For example, sharks and dolphins both have streamlined bodies for swimming.

The Petrified Forest National Park preserves Late Triassic ecosystems, including fossils of early dinosaurs, crocodile relatives, and many plants. Over 3,000 bones have been found at a single quarry there.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Strange growth pattern – starts on four legs, ends on two
└─ specific: 'What if you started life crawling… and then walked upright?'
KEY EVENT: Description of Sonselasuchus cedrus
├─ WHO: Elliott Armour Smith (lead author) and team from Univ. of Washington & Burke Museum
├─ WHEN: Late Triassic, 225–201 million years ago
└─ SIZE: 25 inches tall, size of a poodle
EVIDENCE FOR GROWTH CHANGE: Analysis of fossil leg bones
├─ Hint: hind limbs grew longer and more robust than forelimbs
└─ Quote: 'started on four legs… walked on two legs as they grew up'
COMPARISON TO DINOSAURS: Similar to ornithomimids, but evolved separately
└─ Explanation: convergent evolution due to same ecosystems
OPEN QUESTION: Why did this growth pattern evolve? (not stated in article)

perspectives.description: Researchers now have clear fossil evidence for a major change in locomotion within a single species, opening new avenues for studying growth and evolution.
The conclusions rely on a limited number of fossils from one site; more specimens are needed to confirm whether this pattern was typical for the species.
This case is a textbook example of convergent evolution between croc-line and bird-line archosaurs, reinforcing how environmental pressures can shape similar forms.
Scientists will likely search for more fossils of juvenile and adult shuvosaurids to test whether this growth switch happened in other species, and to understand what drove it.
